revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23989927232|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:غيون (شارع)]][[ca:Gion]][[cs:Gion]][[cy:Gion]][[en:Gion]][[es:Gion]][[eu:Gion barrutia]][[fa:گیون]][[fi:Gion]][[fr:Gion]][[hu:Gion gésanegyed]][[it:Gion]][[ja:祇園]][[ka:გიონი]][[ko:기온 (교토시)]][[my:ဂိအွန်း]][[pt:Gion]][[ru:Гион]][[sa:गिओन्]][[sk:Gion]][[sl:Gion]][[te:జియాన్]][[tr:Gion]][[uk:Ґіон]][[wuu:祇园]][[zh-classical:祇園 (京都市)]][[zh:祇園]]
{{short description|Geisha district in Kyoto}} {{More references|date=April 2021}} {{coord|35.003496|135.775051|display=title}} [[File:150124 Gion Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg|thumb|Hanamikoji street]] [[File:Gion Higashi Tsunemomo.jpg|thumb|Higashi geisha Tsunemomo surrounded by paper lanterns]] {{Nihongo|'''Gion'''|祇園}}{{efn|In {{ill|Historical kana orthography|en|Historical kana orthography|qid=Q1142552|lt=traditional kana spelling|1=Historical kana orthography}}, Gion is spelt {{lang|ja|ぎをん}}, which in modern orthography is {{lang|ja|ぎおん}}. The first kanji in the name's kanji spelling, {{lang|ja|祇}}, is properly as {{lang|ja|示}} + {{lang|ja|氏}}, but is often written as {{lang|ja|ネ}} + {{lang|ja|氏}} instead, and on computers depends on the font used. Occasionally {{lang|ja|'祗'}} ({{lang|ja|示}} + {{lang|ja|氐}}) is seen, but this is a mistake, as these are distinct characters.}} is a district of {{ill|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|en|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1203862|lt=Higashiyama-ku|1=Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, originating as an entertainment district in the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}, in front of [[Yasaka Shrine]] (Gion Shrine). The district was built to accommodate the needs of travellers and visitors to the shrine. It eventually evolved to become one of the most exclusive and well-known {{ill|Hanamachi|en|Hanamachi|qid=Q1574228|lt=geisha districts|1=Hanamachi}} in all of Japan. Gion is the Japanese translation (via Chinese ''Qiyuan'') of the Buddhist term {{ill|Jetavana|en|Jetavana|qid=Q305242|lt=Jetavana|1=Jetavana}}.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Muller|first=Charles|date=2010|title=祇園精舍|url=http://www.buddhism-dict.net/ddb/pcache/79id(b7947-5712-7cbe-820d).html|access-date=2021-08-24|website=Digital Dictionary of Buddhism}}</ref> [[Yasaka Shrine]], located in this district is the center of the [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |last=kyotokankoyagi |date=2021-01-06 |title=Gozu Tenno and Yasaka shrine: The Deity is still alive in the fear of COVID-19 牛頭天王英語で説明 |url=https://kyotokankoyagi.com/gozutenno-en |access-date=2022-06-16 |website=ヤギの京都観光案内/KYOTO GOAT BLOG |language=ja}}</ref>
The {{ill|Geisha|en|Geisha|qid=Q82723|lt=Geisha|1=Geisha}} in Kyoto do not refer to themselves as geisha, instead using the [[Kansai dialect#Regional differences|local term]] {{transl|ja|'geiko'}}. While the term geisha means "artist" or "person of the arts", the more direct term {{transl|ja|geiko}} means essentially "a woman of art".
==Divisions== Gion houses two {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hanamachi|en|Hanamachi|qid=Q1574228|lt=Hanamachi|1=Hanamachi}}}}, or geisha districts: {{nihongo|Gion Kobu|祇園甲部}} and {{nihongo|Gion Higashi|祇園東}}. The two were originally the same district, but split many years ago. Gion Kobu is larger, occupying most of the district including the famous street [[Hanamikoji]], while Gion Higashi is smaller and occupies the northeast corner, centered on its rehearsal hall. Despite the considerable decline in the number of geisha in Gion in the last century, the area is still famous for the preservation of forms of traditional {{ill|Japanese architecture|en|Japanese architecture|qid=Q1422874|lt=architecture|1=Japanese architecture}} and entertainment.
Part of this district has been declared a national historical preservation district. The City of Kyoto has undertaken a number of restorative projects to enhance the beauty and historical authenticity of Kyoto's {{transl|ja|{{ill|Hanamachi|en|Hanamachi|qid=Q1574228|lt=Hanamachi|1=Hanamachi}}}}, such as relocating overhead utilities underground. Since 1986, the city has removed electric poles from a number of popular tourist destinations such as Nene no Michi (ねねの道), [[Hanamikoji]] (花見小路), and Pontocho (先斗町).<ref>{{Cite news |date=2021-11-10 |title=京都・先斗町通すっきり 無電柱化が完了 |work=Sankei News |url=https://www.sankei.com/article/20211110-LWY5Z6IA55NK3OBLX3B4MCKWDI/ |access-date=2022-12-12}}</ref>
<gallery mode="packed" heights="150"> File:Gion.jpg|Street in Gion File:Wooden and bamboo facades of dwellings with sudare in a cobbled street of Gion, perspective effect with vanishing point, Kyoto, Japan.jpg|Dwellings with {{transl|ja|{{ill|Sudare|en|Sudare|qid=Q1456160|lt=Sudare|1=Sudare}}}} in the street leading to the Gion Tatsumi Bridge File:Kyoto gion01.jpg|Along Shirakawa river File:Shijo-dori Kyoto.jpg|View of Shijo-dori in 1886 </gallery>
==Entertainment== [[Image:Shirakara Canal, Gion, Kyoto.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Shirakawa River|en|Shirakawa River|qid=Q7498246|lt=Shirakawa River|1=Shirakawa River}} in the Gion district, showing the rear of some {{transl|ja|{{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|qid=Q7076300|lt=Ochaya|1=Ochaya}}}}|left]]
===Geisha=== [[File:Gion restaurant by vadym777 in Kyoto.jpg|thumb|upright|A typical {{transl|ja|{{ill|Kaiseki|en|Kaiseki|qid=Q175595|lt=Kaiseki|1=Kaiseki}}}} restaurant in Gion]] Gion retains a number of old-style Japanese houses called {{transl|ja|{{ill|Machiya|en|Machiya|qid=Q17222820|lt=Machiya|1=Machiya}}}}, which roughly translates to "townhouse", some of which function as {{transl|ja|{{ill|Ochaya|en|Ochaya|qid=Q7076300|lt=Ochaya|1=Ochaya}}}}, or "teahouses", where geisha entertain guests at parties, involving singing, traditional dance performances, drinking games and conversation. Both geisha and {{transl|ja|{{ill|Maiko|en|Maiko|qid=Q616856|lt=Maiko|1=Maiko}}}} can be seen travelling throughout the district to attend parties, lessons and various other engagements. A number of dressing-up parlors, known as {{transl|ja|henshin}} studios, will dress tourists up as {{transl|ja|maiko}} or geisha for a fee, allowing them to walk through the streets of Gion and have their photo taken; by law in Kyoto, these tourists are required to dress inaccurately, so as not to impersonate geisha or {{transl|ja|maiko}}.
Geisha and {{transl|ja|maiko}} both perform in public dance events staged yearly, such as the {{ill|Miyako Odori|en|Miyako Odori|qid=Q1940917|lt=Miyako Odori|1=Miyako Odori}}; some also perform dances for tourists at Gion Corner.
=== Other === There are also many modern entertainment establishments in Gion – restaurants, bars, clubs, {{ill|Pachinko|en|Pachinko|qid=Q836661|lt=Pachinko|1=Pachinko}}, {{ill|off-track betting|en|off-track betting|qid=Q491916|lt=off-track betting|1=Off-track betting}}, and a very large number of tourist-oriented establishments, particularly along {{ill|Shijō Street|en|Shijō Street|qid=Q7496525|lt=Shijō Street|1=Shijō Street}}; the region is both a major tourist hub, and a popular nightlife spot for locals. Though a number of streets are modern in construction, a number of quieter streets featuring traditional architecture also exist, such as {{Nihongo|{{visible anchor|Hanami Lane}}|花見小路|Hanami-Kōji|"{{ill|Hanami|en|Hanami|qid=Q210150|lt=flower-viewing|1=Hanami}} lane"}} and its environs, ranging from Shijō Street at the north end, anchored by the famous [[:en:Ichiriki Tei|Ichiriki Tei]], and running south to the major temple of [[Kennin-ji]].
The stretch of the {{ill|Shirakawa River|en|Shirakawa River|qid=Q7498246|lt=Shirakawa River|1=Shirakawa River}} before it enters the [[:en:Kamo river|Kamo river]] is also a popular preserved area. It is lined on the south side with traditional establishments which directly face the river, and some are accessed by crossing bridges from the north side. The north side was previously also lined with buildings, but these were torn down in World War II as a fire-prevention measure, and the section is now primarily a pedestrian street, lined with cherry blossoms. These are lit up in the evening in the spring, and the area is active year-round.
==Annual events== [[File:Miyako Odori; cherry dance in Kyoto; 都をどり.jpg|thumbnail|{{ill|Miyako Odori|en|Miyako Odori|qid=Q1940917|lt=Miyako Odori|1=Miyako Odori}}]] The geisha and {{transl|ja|maiko}} of Gion both perform annual public dances, as do all five [[Hanamachi#Kyoto hanamachi|geisha districts in Kyoto]]. The oldest of these date to the [[Kyoto exhibition]] of 1872. The more popular of these is the {{ill|Miyako Odori|en|Miyako Odori|qid=Q1940917|lt=Miyako Odori|1=Miyako Odori}}, literally "Dances of the Old Capital" (sometimes instead referred to as the "Cherry Blossom Dances"), staged by the geisha of Gion Kobu, which dates to 1872. The dances run from April 1 through April 30 each year during the height of the cherry blossom ({{transl|ja|[[:en:Sakura|Sakura]]}}) season. Spectators from Japan and worldwide attend the events, which range from "cheap" seats on [[tatami]] mats on the floor, to reserved seats with a small [[:en:Tea ceremony|Tea ceremony]] beforehand. Gion Higashi holds a similar dance in early November, around autumn leaves, known as Gion Odori; this is more recent and has fewer performances.
==Education== The Kyoto Municipal Board of Education operates public elementary and junior high schools. Gionmachi Kitagawa and Gionmachi Minamigawa are zoned to Kaisei Elementary and Junior High School ([[:ja:東山開睛館|開睛小中学校]]).<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.city.kyoto.lg.jp:443/kyoiku/cmsfiles/contents/0000246/246308/05higashiyama_.pdf|title=京都市通学区町名一覧（東山区）|publisher=City of Kyoto|accessdate=2022-10-18|page=2/6}}</ref>
==In popular culture== * Gion is the setting of much of {{ill|Arthur Golden|en|Arthur Golden|qid=Q432728|lt=Arthur Golden|1=Arthur Golden}}'s novel ''{{ill|Memoirs of a Geisha|en|Memoirs of a Geisha|qid=Q829130|lt=Memoirs of a Geisha|1=Memoirs of a Geisha}}.'' * Gion Kobu was also where {{ill|Mineko Iwasaki|en|Mineko Iwasaki|qid=Q267957|lt=Mineko Iwasaki|1=Mineko Iwasaki}} lived and conducted business as a geisha, as mentioned in her autobiography ''[[:en:Geisha of Gion|Geisha of Gion]].'' * Gion is the setting of several films by {{ill|Kenji Mizoguchi|en|Kenji Mizoguchi|qid=Q55401|lt=Kenji Mizoguchi|1=Kenji Mizoguchi}}, including: ** 1936 {{nihongo|''{{ill|Sisters of the Gion|en|Sisters of the Gion|qid=Q762077|lt=Sisters of the Gion|1=Sisters of the Gion}}''|祇園の姉妹|Gion no kyōdai}} ** 1953 {{nihongo|''{{ill|A Geisha|en|A Geisha|qid=Q862905|lt=A Geisha|1=A Geisha}}'' aka ''Gion Music Festival''|祇園囃子|Gion bayashi}} * Part of the Gion area is featured as a photo travel landscape in the 2010 racing video game {{ill|Gran Turismo 5|en|Gran Turismo 5|qid=Q773395|lt=Gran Turismo 5|1=Gran Turismo 5}}.
==See also== * [[:en:Kyoto hanamachi|Kyoto hanamachi]] * [[:en:Miyagawachō|Miyagawachō]], another district located just south of Gion * [[:en:Pontochō|Pontochō]], another district on west side of Kamo river * {{ill|Kamishichiken|en|Kamishichiken|qid=Q735084|lt=Kamishichiken|1=Kamishichiken}}, the oldest district in Kyoto * [[Gion Matsuri]], one of the largest annual festival in Japan, taking its name from the district
== Notes == {{notelist}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category}} *{{cite web |url=http://www.japan-guide.com/e/e3902.html |title=Kyoto Travel: Gion |access-date=2009-07-05}} *{{cite web|url=http://www.japanvisitor.com/index.php?cID=432&pID=1854|title=Japan Visitor: Gion|access-date=2009-07-05}} *{{cite web |url=http://japantraveladventures.com/kyoto-gion-district-walk-with-geisha-and-maiko/ |title=Kyoto's Gion district – Walk the streets with Geisha and Maiko |access-date=2017-03-22}} *[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jSbRaQsoVy0 Miyako Odori]
[[en:Gion]]
